skill.name: mobile-design
skill.description: Mobile-first design and engineering guidance for iOS and Android apps. It covers touch interaction, performance, platform conventions, offline behavior, mobile backend concerns, testing, and debugging for React Native, Flutter, and native mobile work.
skill.summary: Design mobile apps with touch-first, platform-aware, and performance-conscious guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: AI review found no confirmed malicious behavior. The static command-execution, credential, crypto, C2, and network alerts are false positives from Markdown examples, mobile security guidance, and regex strings inside a local audit script. The only real risk is a Python helper that reads developer-selected project files for local analysis, so publication is acceptable with low-risk metadata.

low_findings.title: Local Project File Audit Script
low_findings.description: The package includes scripts/mobile_audit.py, which accepts a user-provided file or directory and reads React Native, Flutter, and related source files for local mobile UX checks. This is legitimate for an audit helper, but it is a real filesystem-reading capability when run by a user or agent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly opens files and walks directories, but only for a path supplied at runtime. I found no evidence of network transmission, credential theft, destructive writes, or hidden execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick executions. Reviewed examples are Markdown fences, inline commands, or code examples in mobile design documentation rather than executable Ruby code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documentation. The backticks delimit examples such as formulas, adb commands, Gradle commands, and Kotlin snippets; they are not evaluated by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Security Terms Used as Defensive Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: Static alerts for Keychain access, Windows SAM, browser storage, secrets, and certificates are educational text about safe mobile storage and debugging. They do not read host credential stores or extract secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context explicitly warns against insecure token storage and recommends SecureStore, Keychain, or EncryptedSharedPreferences. I found no code that accesses Keychain, SAM, browser storage, or certificate files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Indicators Are Documentation or Regex Checks
dangerous_patterns.description: Hardcoded URLs, IP addresses, and HTTP client keywords appear in mobile examples or regex checks that detect fetch and axios usage in target code. The script itself does not make outbound requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed network references are example universal links, emulator localhost guidance, proxy debugging guidance, and regex strings. No requests, sockets, or upload code were found in the script.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Keyword Alerts Are Contextual False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: Static hits for weak cryptography and C2 terminology come from words such as tokens, HMAC, modes, commands, and design diagrams. The content discusses defensive mobile authentication and UI behavior rather than malware control channels or cryptographic implementation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The terms appear in documentation about token lifecycle, request signing, and color mode testing. I found no cryptographic library calls, obfuscation, command-and-control endpoint, or encoded payload.

content.user_title: Design Mobile Apps with Platform Guidance
content.value_statement: Mobile products fail when teams apply desktop patterns to touch devices. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for mobile-first UX, performance, platform behavior, and offline design.
actual_capabilities: Guides mobile feasibility decisions with the Mobile Feasibility and Risk Index.
Explains touch target sizing, thumb zones, gestures, and haptic feedback patterns.
Compares iOS and Android conventions for navigation, typography, icons, and back behavior.
Provides React Native and Flutter performance guidance for lists, animations, rendering, and logging.
Covers mobile backend topics such as offline queues, token storage, push notifications, and API caching.
Includes a local Python audit helper for React Native and Flutter project checks.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete mobile application framework.
The audit script uses pattern checks and can miss issues that require runtime testing.
Teams still need product requirements, device targets, and accessibility goals before design work.
It does not replace platform documentation, app store review rules, or security review.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Mobile Feature
use_cases.description: Use the feasibility framework to decide if a feature fits the target platform, device constraints, offline needs, and interaction complexity.

use_cases.title: Review Mobile Implementation Quality
use_cases.description: Check React Native or Flutter screens for list performance, touch targets, platform behavior, loading states, and accessibility gaps.

use_cases.title: Align Cross-Platform Design Decisions
use_cases.description: Decide which interaction, navigation, typography, and component choices should match across iOS and Android or diverge by platform.

prompt_templates.title: Check Feature Feasibility
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review before design work starts.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mobile-design skill to evaluate this feature with MFRI. Platform: [iOS, Android, or both]. Framework: [framework]. Feature: [describe feature]. Return the score, risks, and required clarifying questions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Mobile Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this mobile screen description for touch target size, thumb zone placement, gesture alternatives, loading states, error recovery, and platform expectations: [screen description].

prompt_templates.title: Choose Platform Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare iOS and Android expectations for this flow: [flow]. Identify what should be shared, what should diverge, and why. Include navigation, typography, gestures, and back behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Release Readiness Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mobile-design skill as a release checklist for this app area: [feature or module]. Evaluate performance, offline behavior, secure storage, push handling, testing coverage, accessibility, and platform-specific risks.

output_examples.input: Review a checkout screen for a cross-platform mobile app.
output_examples.output: The review flags a high-risk payment flow, recommends bottom-positioned primary actions, requires visible retry states, and separates iOS sheet behavior from Android dialog behavior.

output_examples.input: Evaluate a React Native feed that uses a long scrolling list.
output_examples.output: The guidance recommends a virtualized list, memoized row components, stable item identifiers, image caching, removed production logs, and device testing on lower-end phones.

output_examples.input: Plan offline behavior for a messaging app.
output_examples.output: The response proposes cached conversations, queued sends, clear sync status, conflict handling, secure token storage, and graceful recovery after network interruption.

best_practices: Define platform, framework, device class, navigation model, and offline needs before designing screens.
Read the universal mobile guidance and the relevant platform file before making UI decisions.
Validate designs on real devices for touch comfort, performance, accessibility, and network loss.
anti_patterns: Applying desktop patterns such as hover states, small targets, dense tables, and top-heavy actions to phones.
Using one identical UI for iOS and Android when platform navigation or gestures should differ.
Treating offline behavior, secure storage, and low-end device performance as late polish items.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps AI agents and teams avoid desktop-first mobile designs. It makes touch, platform, performance, offline, and accessibility constraints explicit.

faq.question: Does it support both iOS and Android?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes shared mobile principles and separate guidance for iOS and Android platform conventions.

faq.question: Can it help with React Native and Flutter?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for React Native, Flutter, and native mobile patterns, especially around lists, rendering, animations, and testing.

faq.question: Does the skill generate final visual designs?
faq.answer: No. It provides design doctrine, checklists, decision trees, and review criteria that users apply to specific screens or code.

faq.question: What does the included audit script do?
faq.answer: The script reads a selected project path and reports mobile UX, performance, accessibility, testing, and storage concerns using pattern checks.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found no malicious behavior. The package has low risk because it includes a local filesystem-reading audit script.
